documents.name: Dairy Farming - When planning to start dairy cattle farming verify
documents.text:
When planning to start dairy cattle farming verify the following five preconditions.
1. Climate Suitability:*Make sure that the climate is suitable for the selected  dairy breeds. Crossbred dairy cattle (crossed with high-yielding exotic breeds) adapt more easily and provide better yields in the highland (dega) and mid-highland (weyna dega) areas than in the lowlands (kolla). However, indigenous breeds can thrive in all of these ecosystem. 
2. Infrastructure and Services: Ensure the availability of the necessary infrastructure and services for dairy farming. This includes verifying a sustainable feed and water supply, market accessibility, road access, and animal health and breeding services.

3. Equipment: Prepare essential dairy operation utensils and equipment, such as milk measures, milking pails, filters, and feeding and watering  troughs. 
3. Equipment: Prepare essential dairy utensils and equipment, such as milk measures, milking buckets, strainers, and feed and water troughs.
4. Site Drainage: Ensure the farming site is not swampy and is either level or has a slight slope to prevent waterlogging. 
5. Initial Stock:. Confirm the presence of initial stock or foundation animals for your dairy farm. Specifically, check for the availability of improved dairy cattle or upgrading the local breeds within your own household or the surrounding community to serve as your starting herd.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - The following factors must be considered when buil
documents.text:
The following factors must be considered when building a dairy cattle house: 
The long side of the roof should run East to West to prevent direct sunlight from entering the house. 
In Highland and Mid-highland areas, the wall height should be 4.6 meters (9 cubits), and in hot areas up to 5 meters (10 cubits). The floor should have a 2.5% slope and a non-slip surface for easy cleaning. 
The house should protect from sun, rain, and wind while allowing air and light. This can be achieved through windows or a screened open space under the roof along the wall length. Ensure a walkway for workers of at least 1 meter and keep the area free of sharp objects that could injure the cattle. 
The floor should be non-slip, comfortable for cows to lie on, and designed to ensure rapid drainage and prevent liquid retention.
Dimensions for Highland/Mid-highland housing (standing and lying area):
Bulls: 2 meters (4 cubits) length by 1.2 meters (2.5 cubits) width. 
Cows: 1.75 meters (3.5 cubits) length by 1.2 meters (1.5 cubits) width. 
Heifers: 1.5 meters (3 cubits) length by 1 meter (2 cubits) width. 
Calves: 1 meter (2 cubits) length by 1 meter (2 cubits) width. 
In Lowland areas, build an open-stall system without partitioned standing/lying areas, allowing 3.5 square meters per animal. 
Build a drainage channel 20 cm deep and 15 cm wide, with an appropriate slope to prevent the stagnation of urine and other liquids.
Feeding Trough Dimensions:
Cows/Bulls: 0.6 meters (2 cubits) width, 0.4 meters depth, and 1.20 meters length. 
Heifers: 0.5 meters width, 0.4 meters depth, and 1 meter length. 
Calves: 0.4 meters width, 0.15 meters depth, and 1 meter (2 cubits) length. 
Maternity/Sick pens should be 4 meters (8 cubits) by 4 meters (8 cubits) with fully covered, clean walls. The floor should be bedded with clean grass or straw, and sufficient clean water must be provided. 
Separate rooms for feed storage and equipment storage are required. 
Regarding Manure Management and Utilization:
If manure and urine are not properly managed, moisture and ammonia gas levels increase, reducing the cattle's feed intake. It also creates breeding grounds for flies and pathogens; therefore, clean the barn daily and clean feed and water troughs two to three times a week. 
Appropriately store cattle waste, which contains valuable nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, to be used as organic fertilizer. Alternatively, collect it in a sealed pit for anaerobic fermentation to produce biogas as a renewable energy source.
To prepare compost: 
Prepare at least two pits in a shaded area with convenient water access. 
Gather decomposable materials such as cattle manure slurry, feed leftovers, barn sweepings, and dry/wet plant matter. 
Add the decomposable materials to the pit sequentially. Ensure the amount of material added balances the moisture of the manure slurry. 
After 2–3 weeks when the first pit is full, move the manure to the second pit to allow the first to fully ferment. Use the fermented compost as fertilizer.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Exotic breeds or their crossbreeds are known for h
documents.text:
Exotic breeds or their crossbreeds are known for high milk production. 
Holstein Friesian: Black and white or red and white, no hump. With good management, they can yield 30 liters of milk per day and can be milked for 10 consecutive months. 
Jersey: Brown or black with white spots, prominent eyes. They yield over 20 liters per day and can be milked for 10 consecutive months. 
Crossbreeds: Cows resulting from crossing exotic breeds with local breeds can yield 8–10 liters per day and be milked for 10 consecutive months. 
If exotic or crossbred cows/heifers are unavailable, select local breeds known for better milk production in the area. 
Selection by Physical Conformation: Choose cows with a triangular body shape (narrower at the neck, wider at the flanks), wide belly, wide spacing between ribs, long legs, and a large, well-attached udder with large teats. 
When selecting local breeds for improvement, consider good stature, health status, and age. 
Selecting Bulls: Choose bulls that are free from reproductive diseases, have long straight legs, two visible testicles, are neither too fat nor too thin, and are up to five years old. 
If the area has a raw milk market, balanced feed, and health services, Holstein Friesian crossbreeds are appropriate for their high yield. If the area is hot and raw milk markets are inconvenient, Jersey crossbreeds are better as their milk has higher fat content, suitable for processing and selling as butter.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Identifying signs of oestrus ("heat") and performi
documents.text:
Identifying signs of oestrus ("heat") and performing timely breeding are essential for maintaining proper calving intervals and ensuring consistent milk and calf production.
Signs of Heat: Restlessness, bellowing, mounting other cattle, standing to be mounted ("standing heat"), sniffing other cows' genitalia, a reddened and moist vulva, clear/white mucus discharge, a raised tail base, and a temporary decrease in appetite.
Heat Cycle & Timing: A healthy, non-pregnant cow cycles into oestrus approximately every 21 days. As oestrus lasts only about 18 hours, optimal breeding time is 8 to 12 hours after the first observed signs (or towards the end of the standing heat period).
Breeding Method: Use Artificial Insemination (AI) with quality semen if available; otherwise, use a carefully selected natural bull. 
Pregnancy Diagnosis: diagnose the dairy cow with a veterinarian or technician for pregnancy via rectal palpation or ultrasound 2 to 3 months after insemination or natural mating. 
Post-Calving & Infertility: Cows need a minimum 60-day recovery period after calving before re-breeding. A cow should be inseminated after two months period. If a cow fails to conceive after three consecutive, well-timed breeding attempts, consult a veterinarian to diagnose potential infertility issues.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Once pregnancy is confirmed, prevent the cow from
documents.text:
Once pregnancy is confirmed, prevent the cow from traveling long distances or rough surfaces and ensure the barn floor is safe to prevent falls. Slippery floors can cause fractures or dislocations. Prevent dogs or children from chasing them, protect them from being gored by other animals, and keep them away from animals that have recently aborted. 
Once pregnancy is confirmed, prevent the cow from traveling long or rough distances and ensure that the barn floor is safe to prevent falls. Slippery floors can cause fractures or dislocations. Prevent dogs or children from chasing the cow, protect her from being gored by other animals, and keep her away from animals that have recently aborted.
During the first three months of pregnancy, provide sufficient clean water and palatable hay or green fodder.
In the last two months of pregnancy, add about 2 kg of balanced concentrate feed to her daily ration. If concentrate is unavailable, provide high-quality wilted green grass or good-quality forage.
Stop milking (dry off) the cow or heifer gradually once she reaches seven months of pregnancy.
One to two weeks before calving, the udder begins to soften or "shine." One day before calving, signs include restlessness, seeking isolation, swelling of the vulva with mucus discharge, and udder enlargement. A few minutes before calving, the water bag (amniotic sac) will break. 
Prepare the calving area with straw or hay bedding and move the cow or heifer to the calving area.
Follow and monitor the cow regularly in calving area. 
Calving is a natural process; allow the cow to give birth on her own. Seek veterinary assistance if labor is prolonged or if the calf is in an abnormal position.
Allow the cow to lick the calf after birth; if she doesn't, dry the calf with a clean cloth. Burn or bury the afterbirth (placenta) immediately. 
Tie the umbilical cord with clean thread, cut it, and dip it using alcohol or iodine solution. Clean the cow’s external genitalia and tail with lukewarm clean water, and then provide her with grass and lukewarm drinking water to allow her to rest.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Monitor breathing immediately after birth and clea
documents.text:
Monitor breathing immediately after birth and clear mucus from the nose. If the calf is not breathing, gently stimulate breathing by rubbing the body, tickling the nostrils with soft grass, or briefly lifting the calf by the hind legs to help drain fluids. 
Dip the umbilical stump in alcohol or iodine solution to prevent infection.
*Colostrum (*Enger*):  Ensure calves receive at least 0.5 liters within the first 2 hours and up to 3 liters within 24 hours, as it contains essential antibodies. 
Calf pens should be comfortable, non-slip, clean, dry, and warm, as calves cannot tolerate wind and cold. Provide approximately 1 m × 1 m space per calf, with clean straw or hay bedding that is changed regularly.
Isolate sick calves promptly from health one and seek close veterinary supervision regularly. To prevent calf scours (diarrhea), ensure milk, pails, and feeding troughs are clean and properly sanitized.
Make sure that both the housing and the grazing area are clean.
Consult a veterinarian regarding vaccination and parasite control programs, including protection against internal and external parasites, especially at the beginning and end of the rainy season.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Colostrum (*Enger*): Since colostrum contains esse
documents.text:
Colostrum (*Enger*): Since colostrum contains essential antibodies, ensure the calf suckles directly from the mother or is fed milked colostrum within the first 2 hours after birth (at least 0.5 liters) and up to 3 liters within the first 24 hours. Feed colostrum three times a day for at least 3–5 consecutive days.
Emergency Colostrum: If the cow dies during birth, feed the newborn colostrum milked from another cow in the herd that gave birth at the same time. 
Artificial Colostrum: If no other nursing cow is available, prepare artificial colostrum by mixing one egg into 1 liter of milk, plus one "soft drink" bottle (approx. 0.5L) of warm water, half a liter of milk, and half a teaspoon of cooking oil. Feed this three times a day for at least 4 consecutive days. 
Feeding Schedule (Birth to 4 Months):
5 days to 2 months: Feed 3 liters of milk daily (split equally between morning and evening) plus one handful of balanced calf starter feed. 
2 months to 3 months: Feed 2 liters of milk twice a day and 200 grams of concentrate feed. 
3 months to 4 months: Feed 0.5 liters of milk twice a day and 2 kg of concentrate feed. 
From day 5 until 4 months, provide clean grass and water as desired (*ad libitum*). 
Weaning:  Calves are typically weaned (stop drinking milk) at 4 months of age, but only after ensuring they are healthy and in good physical condition. 
4 months to 12 months: Feed an average of 2 kg of balanced concentrate feed daily, plus as much grass and forage as they can consume.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Pre-breeding: Pre-breeding: For dairy heifers aged
documents.text:
Pre-breeding: Pre-breeding: For dairy heifers aged 1 year to 1.5 years (or until they reach breeding age), provide high-quality hay or grass/forage and clean drinking water. If the available feed is of low quality, supplement with 1 kg of balanced heifer concentrate per day.
Early Pregnancy: From the day a heifer conceives until 7 months of pregnancy, provide high-quality hay or green fodder and water. Supplement with 1 kg of concentrate if quality fodder is unavailable. 
Late Pregnancy:  From 7 months of pregnancy until calving, provide 2 kg of balanced concentrate daily in addition to hay or green fodder and sufficient clean water.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - *Lactation: From the day of calving until the 7th
documents.text:
*Lactation: From the day of calving until the 7th month of the next pregnancy, provide balanced roughage such as hay, pasture, “gefera” (milling by-products), or straw, along with sufficient clean water. 
Production Supplement: Additionally, feed 0.5 kg of dairy concentrate daily for every 1 liter of milk produced. 
Dry Period: After 7 months of pregnancy, stop milking (“dry off”) and provide 2 kg of balanced concentrate daily in addition to the roughage and water until calving. 
Feed Safety: Ensure feed does not become moldy due to moisture, as this can spoil the milk and harm the cattle. Maximum care should be given during feed storage to prevent contamination and feed spoilage. Do not give contaminated and spoiled feed to the cow and carefully dispose of any spoiled feed.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Environment: Prepare a quiet, clean milking area w
documents.text:
Environment: Prepare a quiet, clean milking area with adequate ventilation and light. Use a consistent milking time and a healthy, regular milker. 
 Preparation: Before milking, sanitize all equipment. Move the cow to a clean, quiet milking area. The milker should wear clean clothes and boots. Providing a small amount of feed helps keep the cow calm.
Hygiene: Wash hands with soap and clean water. Clean the cow’s udder and teats with a dedicated, single-use disinfectant wipe or a sanitizing solution, then dry them thoroughly with a clean towel.
Testing: Milk the first 3-4 streams from each teat into a strip cup or dark surface to check for abnormalities (clots, flakes, watery consistency) that indicate mastitis. Discard this milk. 
Milking: Complete the milking quickly and hygienically. Avoid loud noises, hitting, or rushing the cow, and keep flies and insects away during milking time. 
Post-Milking: Measure and record the yield of the milk. Wash and dry the udder and teats again. Filter the milk immediately through a thin white cloth (muslin) to remove debris. 
Storage:  Keep milk in a cool place if it cannot be delivered immediately to consumers, milk collector or processors. 
Use stainless steel or aluminum for milk containers and transportation. 

Handling: Do not mix morning and evening milk, as evening milk has been stored longer and has a higher bacterial count, which can lower the quality of fresh morning milk. 
Processing: If a reliable market is unavailable, use small-scale processing to turn milk into butter, cheese (“ayib”), or ice cream to extend shelf life and add value. 
During transportation of milk, use cold chain and care should be given.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Cause:  Mastitis is an inflammation of the udder c
documents.text:
Cause:  Mastitis is an inflammation of the udder caused by different  bacterial infection. It reduces milk yield and quality, can lead to teat blockage, and if left untreated, can become chronic or fatal.
Transmission: The disease is transmitted primarily during milking through contact with contaminated equipment, milker's hands, or bedding. A lack of hygiene and failure to isolate infected cows facilitates its spread.

However, maintain udder hygiene and sanitation, treat infected cow , disinfection of the barn, isolation of infected cow, maintain miller’s hygiene  can minimize and control the disease. 
Symptoms: Swollen, firm, or hot udder; pain upon palpation; redness; systemic signs like fever and anorexia; and abnormal milk (watery, clotted, or containing blood or pus).
Prevention: Maintain strict milking hygiene and barn sanitation. Mastitis spreads through contaminated equipment, unwashed hands, and unsanitary condition
Management: Provide a clean, dry, and well-ventilated stall. Remove manure regularly and ensure proper drainage. Thoroughly sanitize all milking equipment and use a separate, clean towel for each cow.
Action:  Upon detecting symptoms, immediately isolate the cow and consult a veterinarian. Milk the infected cow frequently (three times daily) and safely discard the milk to prevent environmental contamination. All treatment must be administered under professional guidance using appropriate antibiotics.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Nature of the Disease: Brucellosis is a highly con
documents.text:
Nature of the Disease: Brucellosis is a highly contagious zoonotic bacterial disease, primarily caused in cattle by Brucella abortus. It is a major cause of reproductive failure, characterized most commonly by late-term abortions (typically after the 5th-7th month of gestation). The disease leads to substantial economic losses due to abortions, reduced milk yield, infertility, and the birth of weak or stillborn calves. As a significant zoonosis, it poses a serious health risk to humans (Undulant Fever).
Clinical Signs: The cardinal clinical sign is late-term abortion in pregnant cows and heifers. Infected adult animals are often asymptomatic carriers, showing no outward signs. Other potential signs include:
Retained placenta (fetal membranes)
Purulent (pus-like) vulvar discharge
Metritis (uterine infection)
Orchitis and epididymitis (testicular inflammation) in bulls, leading to infertility
Arthritis (joint swelling) and burstis in chronic cases
A persistent drop in milk production
Transmission: The primary routes of infection are: Ingestion of feed or water contaminated with aborted fetuses, fetal membranes, or uterine discharges.
direct contact with infected animals or their bodily fluids (e.g., during calving or abortion).Venereal transmission via infected bulls or contaminated artificial insemination equipment. Zoonotic transmission to humans occurs through consumption of raw (unpasteurized) milk and dairy products, or through direct contact with infected tissues and fluids via mucous membranes or broken skin.

Prevention and Control:
Test and Cull: Implement a rigorous test-and-slaughter policy. All new animals must undergo serological testing and quarantine before introduction to the herd. Chronically infected or aborting animals must be permanently removed from the herd.
Vaccination: In endemic areas, vaccinate heifer calves with vaccines as per veterinary guidance. 

3. Biosecurity:

Isolate aborting cows immediately.
Disinfect calving areas and all equipment with a bactericidal disinfectant (e.g., iodine-based).
Handle aborted fetuses, placentas, and discharges with extreme caution using personal protective equipment (PPE) such as gloves and masks. Incinerate or deeply bury these materials.
Ensure all cattle entering the farm have been medically tested and confirmed free of the disease.
4. Herd Management:
Use only brucellosis-free bulls or certified semen for breeding.
Maintain excellent hygiene in barns and milking parlors.
• Zoonotic Protection (Human Safety):
Never consume raw (unpasteurized) milk or dairy products from herds of unknown status.
Always wear impermeable gloves, masks, and protective clothing when handling animals during calving, abortion, or when dealing with discharges.
Practice stringent personal hygiene: Wash hands and arms thoroughly with soap and disinfectant after any animal contact.

Immediately report any suspected cases in animals to veterinary authorities and any flu-like symptoms in humans after exposure to a physician, mentioning possible brucellosis exposure.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Nature of the Disease: Foot and Mouth Disease is c
documents.text:
Nature of the Disease: Foot and Mouth Disease is caused by a virus .It is a highly contagious and economically devastating disease that affects cloven-hoofed animals, causing lesions in the mouth, on the feet, and on the udder and teats, leading to severe drops in productivity.
Transmission: The virus spreads through aerosols (airborne transmission), contaminated fomites (objects like equipment, vehicles, and clothing), feed, water, and people. It is also shed in all excretions and secretions, including milk, saliva, urine, feces, and semen, facilitating both direct and indirect contact transmission.
Zoonotic Note: While extremely rare, the virus can infect humans; however, it does not cause significant illness. Strict biosecurity and hygiene measures are essential for handlers to prevent mechanical spread.
Symptoms: The primary symptom is the appearance of vesicles (fluid-filled blisters) on the tongue, gums, dental pad, lips, coronary bands of the hooves, and teats. Immediately upon suspicion, isolate the animal and contact veterinary authorities. Subsequent signs include ruptured vesicles forming painful erosions and ulcers, severe lameness, profuse salivation, nasal discharge, fever, anorexia, and a drastic drop in milk yield ("milk drop syndrome"). Pregnant animals may abort.
Treatment and Care: There is no specific antiviral treatment for FMD. Veterinary care is supportive and palliative, aimed at preventing secondary bacterial infections in the lesions, managing pain, and maintaining hydration and nutrition. Any suspected case must be reported to government veterinary services immediately.
• Supportive Home Care: Isolate affected animals. Gently clean mouth and foot lesions with a mild antiseptic solution (e.g., diluted potassium permanganate). Applying emollients like honey or glycerin can aid healing and provide a protective barrier. Provide soft, palatable feed and easy access to clean water.
• Prevention: Vaccination is a key control measure in endemic regions. The cornerstone of prevention is strict biosecurity: controlling animal movement, disinfecting equipment and premises, and maintaining a quarantine period for new animals.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Nature of the Disease: Lumpy Skin Disease (LSD) is
documents.text:
Nature of the Disease: Lumpy Skin Disease (LSD) is a highly contagious, vector-borne, and economically significant transboundary disease of cattle, caused by the Lumpy Skin Disease Virus (LSDV) of the Poxviridae family. Outbreaks are often seasonal, peaking during and after the rainy season when populations of insect vectors (mosquitoes, biting flies) are highest. Clinical signs include the development of characteristic firm, raised skin nodules on the head, neck, limbs, udder, and perineum. These nodules can ulcerate, leading to severe secondary bacterial infections. Systemic illness causes high fever, lymphadenopathy, pronounced milk drop (agalactia) in lactating cows, temporary infertility in bulls, and respiratory distress, resulting in significant morbidity, mortality, and economic loss.
Transmission: LSD is primarily transmitted mechanically by blood-feeding arthropod vectors, including mosquitoes (Culicoides spp.), biting flies (e.g., stable flies), and ticks. It can also spread through direct contact with lesions, secretions, or contaminated fomites (feed, water, equipment) and iatrogenically via contaminated needles or surgical instruments.
Clinical Signs: The pathognomonic sign is the eruption of multiple cutaneous nodules over the entire body. These nodules involve the full thickness of the skin (dermis) and can develop into deep, necrotic ulcers. Other systemic signs include pyrexia (high fever), depression, anorexia, weight loss, lameness, lymphadenitis, and mucopurulent ocular and nasal discharge. Survivors may have permanent scarring or hide damage as nodules slough. Any suspected case must be reported immediately to veterinary authorities.
Treatment and Care: There is no specific antiviral treatment for LSD. Management is strictly supportive and involves controlling secondary bacterial infections. Isolate all clinically affected animals immediately. Treatment includes:
- Systemic broad-spectrum antibiotics to treat/prevent secondary infections.
- Anti-inflammatory drugs to reduce fever and pain.
- Topical treatment of ulcers with antiseptics (e.g., povidone-iodine) and wound sprays.
- Supportive care: Provide soft feed, easy access to water, and clean, dry bedding.
• Prevention and Control:
- Vaccination: The most effective preventive measure is prophylactic vaccination of all susceptible cattle in at-risk areas using a live-attenuated homologous vaccine.
- Vector Control: Implement insect control programs (insecticides, repellents, environmental management).
- Biosecurity: Enforce strict movement controls, quarantine new animals, and disinfect premises and equipment.
- Culling and Disposal: In some control programs, severe cases may require humane culling and safe carcass disposal to break the transmission cycle.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Nature of the Condition: Calcium deficiency, clini
documents.text:
Nature of the Condition: Calcium deficiency, clinically known as Milk Fever or Parturient Paresis, is a metabolic disorder affecting dairy cows primarily around the time of calving (periparturient period). It is characterized by hypocalcemia (low blood calcium), leading to muscular weakness, nervous system depression, and recumbency. This condition critically impairs calving and the initiation of lactation. Although it can affect cows of any age, it is most prevalent in older, high-producing multiparous cows. Without prompt veterinary intervention, it is often fatal.
Cause: Cause: The primary cause is a sudden and severe drain on blood calcium at the onset of lactation, as large amounts of calcium are secreted into colostrum and milk. Contributing factors include:
An inadequate dietary intake of calcium and/or vitamin D during the dry period.
A dietary cation-anion imbalance during the late dry period that impairs the cow's ability to mobilize calcium from bone reserves.
Higher genetic merit for milk production, making exotic crossbreeds more susceptible than indigenous breeds.
Management systems with zero grazing, which can limit feed variety and sunlight exposure (for vitamin D synthesis).
Symptoms:
Before calving: Drowsiness, increased body temperature, occasional restlessness, inability to stand up, and inability to give birth.
Within 72 hours after calving: Partial or full paralysis or inability to rise; cows lie on their chests or sides and are unable to lift their heads. Uterine prolapse and retained placenta are also signs. Symptoms:
Early Stage (Prodromal): Drowsiness, ataxia (staggering), restlessness, muscle tremors, and a cold body temperature.
Stage 1 (Sternal Recumbency): The cow is unable to stand, lies on her chest ("downer cow") with her head turned into her flank, and may exhibit gastrointestinal stasis (bloat, no defecation).
Stage 2 (Lateral Recumbency): The cow lies flat on her side, exhibits muscle flaccidity, a weak pulse, and a subnormal body temperature. Uterine prolapse and retained fetal membranes are common complications.
Treatment: This is a veterinary emergency. Treatment consists of a slow, intravenous infusion of a calcium borogluconate solution. Do not delay. For cows that have recently calved, reduce the calcium drain by stripping out only a small amount of colostrum/milk instead of fully milking them out until calcium levels are restored.

Prevention: Implement a pre-calving (close-up) dietary management program. This includes feeding a low-calcium, high-fiber diet during the far-off dry period and a negative dietary cation-anion difference (DCAD) diet in the 3-4 weeks pre-calving. Ensure access to high-quality forages and provide appropriate mineral supplements.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Tuberculosis is a chronic, highly contagious, and
documents.text:
Tuberculosis is a chronic, highly contagious, and zoonotic disease of dairy cattle. It is caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium bovis and leads to progressive emaciation (wasting) and severe economic losses due to reduced milk production, condemnation of carcasses, and trade restrictions. The disease is widespread and poses a significant risk to human health.
Transmission:
Primarily through inhalation of aerosolized bacteria from infected animals (coughing, contaminated dust).
Direct contact with infected animals, their secretions, or contaminated products (e.g., unpasteurized milk).
Indirect contact via contaminated equipment, clothing, or feed.
Feeding contaminated raw (unpasteurized) milk to calves or humans, leading to infection.
Symptoms: 
Chronic, hacking cough that worsens with exercise.
Labored breathing and respiratory distress.
Animals may stand with an extended neck and arched back.
Thick, purulent nasal discharge.
Loss of appetite (anorexia), persistent thirst, and progressive emaciation despite normal feed intake.
In advanced stages, enlarged, palpable lymph nodes (especially in the neck).
Prevention and Control:
Implement strict biosecurity for farm workers: use protective clothing, practice good hygiene, and limit contact.
Provide optimal husbandry: sufficient clean water, nutritionally balanced feed, and proper shelter to reduce stress.
Consult a veterinarian for a health assessment prior to purchasing any new cattle.
Require a negative tuberculin test (or other approved diagnostic test) for all newly introduced animals.
Permanently remove (cull) animals that show clinical signs or test positive for the disease, in accordance with national eradication program guidelines.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Record keeping is a fundamental practice and indic
documents.text:
Record keeping is a fundamental practice and indicator of professional dairy farming. Verbal information is easily forgotten or miscommunicated; therefore, systematic documentation is essential for calculating profit and loss, making informed management decisions, and identifying areas for operational improvement.
Individual Record: 
Maintain a lifetime file for each animal from birth/entry until exit from the herd. Essential data to include:
- Animal Identification (ID) Number or Name
- Date of Birth or Date of Purchase
- Breed and Genetic Lineage
- Calving History: Dates of all calvings
- Lactation Data: Duration of each milking period, daily milk yield, and 305-day lactation average
- Nutrition: Type and quantity of feed provided
- Reproductive History: Date of first observed heat, dates of breeding or Artificial Insemination (AI) (including sire/bull ID), pregnancy diagnosis date, expected calving date, actual calving date, and drying-off date
- Exit Information: Age and date of removal from the herd, and reason for removal (e.g., sale, culling, death)
Calf Record: Track the growth and health of all young stock.  Key data points:
Calf ID Number
Date of Birth and Sex
Sire (Father) and Dam (Mother) ID
Birth Weight, Weaning Weight, and Weaning Age
Health Protocol: Dates and types of vaccinations administered, medical treatments, and parasite control measures
Financial and General Farm Record: Maintain comprehensive records of all farm inputs and outputs for financial analysis.
- Feed Inventory: Type, quantity, and cost of feed produced on-farm and purchased; record of which animals consumed specific feed batches.
- Capital & Operational Costs: Farm establishment costs, labor expenses, inventory of fixed (land, buildings) and movable assets (equipment), and veterinary/medicine costs.
                      - Income: Revenue from the sale of milk, calves, culled animals, manure,   
                       and other by-products.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Sales Channels: If road access is suitable, sell m
documents.text:
Sales Channels: If road access is suitable, sell milk directly to dairies or processing plants. If direct sales are not feasible, deliver milk to established dairy cooperatives or milk collection centers. Entering into long-term supply contracts with buyers helps stabilize income and reduces market price volatility.
Transportation & Quality: Transport milk in clean, food-grade stainless steel or aluminum containers that are light-proof (e.g., milk churns, "Mazican"). Containers should have wide openings for thorough cleaning and inspection. Adulteration (adding water or foreign substances) is strictly prohibited as it compromises public health and severely downgrades quality. Maintain milk quality by keeping it clean, covered, and cooled immediately after milking to ensure a premium price.
Surplus Sales (Livestock & Feed): Sell surplus heifers at optimal market prices. Cull and sell older cows with declining production and male calves not intended for breeding to local rearers or feedlot operators. Sell any excess fodder or conserved feed to generate additional farm revenue.
Cost Management: Purchase feed inputs (e.g., wheat bran, maize middlings, oilseed cakes like noug cake) in bulk during periods of low market prices to reduce overall feed costs.
Input Quality & Safety: Purchase all veterinary pharmaceuticals, vaccines, and chemicals exclusively from licensed and accredited veterinary suppliers. Avoid expired, counterfeit, or substandard products to prevent animal harm, drug resistance, and milk contamination.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Dairy Farming - Manure Management: Improper handling of manure and
documents.text:
Manure Management: Improper handling of manure and urine elevates moisture levels and ammonia (NH₃) concentrations in the barn environment. This compromises air quality, suppresses cattle feed intake (dry matter consumption), and creates ideal breeding sites for flies and pathogens (bacteria and viruses). To mitigate this, thoroughly clean the barn daily and scrub feed and water troughs 2–3 times per week to remove organic residue.
 Nutrient Utilization: Cattle manure is a valuable source of the primary macronutrients Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K).
As Fertilizer: Properly compost and store manure to produce stable organic fertilizer for crop production.
As Energy: Collect manure in an anaerobic digester (biogas plant) to produce methane-rich biogas through fermentation, providing a renewable energy source for cooking or electricity.
 Carcass Disposal: Animals that die from infectious disease must be removed and disposed of immediately to break the disease transmission cycle and prevent environmental contamination.
Recommended Methods: Bury carcasses in a deep, secure pit (at least 2 meters deep) located away from water sources, or incinerate them.
Biosecurity: After removal, thoroughly disinfect the contaminated area and any equipment used with a veterinary-grade disinfectant.
documents.source: Small-scale Improved Dairy Cattle Farming Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - Content of the message to be transmitted to the fa
documents.text: Content of the message to be transmitted to the farmer through the Interactive Voice Response (IVR) system:
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - The final tillage operation should break the soil
documents.text:
The final tillage operation should break the soil down into fine, crumbly particles. The ideal seedbed is free of large clods but is not pulverized into dust. The field should be as level as possible to prevent waterlogging.

On sloped land (2-5% gradient), the risk of erosion and water runoff should be managed by constructing drainage channels along the contour, running perpendicular to the slope.

For land under continuous cultivation, plowing 3 to 4 times is generally sufficient and cost-effective, depending on soil type. For virgin or long-term fallow land, more intensive preparation of up to 5 plowings may be necessary.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - The choice of maize variety depends on your field'
documents.text:
The choice of maize variety depends on your field's altitude and rainfall pattern.
For lowland areas with low rainfall: Sow early-maturing varieties that reach maturity in 90 to 110 days. Examples include Melkassa 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6Q, MH130, MH138Q, MH140, and MH141.
For mid-highland (Weyna Dega) areas with sufficient rainfall: Sow varieties with a medium maturity period of 140 to 155 days. Recommended options are BH546, BH547, BH-QPMY545, Nada (BH520 W1), and Shankura (BH5212). You may also consider Belen (BHA5211), Meta (BH5211), BH-QPM548, or Ilu (BH549).
For mid-altitude to highland areas (1600 to 2200 meters above sea level) with sufficient rainfall: Sow the late-maturing variety BH661, which matures in approximately 160 days.
For highland areas with sufficient rainfall: Sow late-maturing varieties such as AMH852Q, AMH853, Ambo (AMH854), Wenchi, and Jibat.
Depending on local rainfall, you can also choose varieties with enhanced protein and Provitamin A content as alternatives. These include Melkassa 6Q, MH138Q, BH-QPMY545, BH-QPM548, Belen (BHA5211), and AMH852Q.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - The recommended seed rate is 25 to 30 kilograms pe
documents.text: The recommended seed rate is 25 to 30 kilograms per hectare. This amount may vary depending on the maize variety and seed grain size.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - The recommended sowing depth for maize ranges from
documents.text:
The recommended sowing depth for maize ranges from 5 cm to 12 cm, depending on soil type.
Heavy clay soil (“Merere”): 5 to 7 cm
Light clay soil and heavy silt soil: 9 cm
Light silt soil: 10 cm
Sandy soil: 12 cm
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - The process of transplanting maize seedlings invol
documents.text: The process of transplanting maize seedlings involves preparing a nursery bed, sowing seeds in a controlled environment, and later moving the healthy young plants to the main field after about 2 to 3 weeks. This method can ensure uniform stands, extend the growing season, and reduce risks from early-season pests or adverse weather. For detailed steps on nursery management and transplanting techniques, please remain on the line to speak with an extension officer.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - Moisture retention is critical for maize growth, e
documents.text: Moisture retention is critical for maize growth, especially in areas with low or erratic rainfall. Techniques such as mulching, tied ridges, in-situ rainwater harvesting, and conservation tillage help reduce evaporation and increase water availability to the crop. For recommendations specific to your soil type and local conditions, please remain on the line to speak with an extension officer.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - In Ethiopia, two main types of fertilizer are used
documents.text:
In Ethiopia, two main types of fertilizer are used to improve maize production: synthetic (inorganic) and organic fertilizers.

For maize cultivation in Ethiopia, studies confirm the use of two primary synthetic fertilizers: Urea and NPS. Application rates depend on the soil's fertility and local rainfall patterns.




The standard recommendations for Urea and NPS fertilizers are based on local soil and climate research. For areas with sufficient rainfall in the mid-altitudes, applying 150–200 kg of NPS and 200–250 kg of Urea per hectare is recommended to increase maize production for common soil types.

For moisture-stressed areas, the recommended rate is 100 kg of NPS and 150 kg of Urea per hectare.

In Oromia and SNNP regions (mid-altitude, moist Weyna Dega and lowland areas):
Apply 150 kg of NPS and 250 kg of Urea per hectare.

In the Amhara region (mid-altitude, moist Weyna Dega and lowland areas):
Apply 200 kg of NPS and 300 kg of Urea per hectare.

For moisture-stressed areas nationwide:
Apply 100 kg of NPS and 150 kg of Urea per hectare.

Organic fertilizers are a valuable resource. For best results:

Compost, bio-slurry, or fermented manure should be spread 3 to 4 weeks before sowing to allow for proper decomposition.

Vermicompost can be applied 1 to 2 weeks before sowing, as its nutrients are readily available.

Recommended application rates per hectare are:

Compost: 80–120 quintals

Bio-slurry compost: 40–60 quintals

Fermented manure: 100–150 quintals

Vermicompost: 20–30 quintals

Combining organic and synthetic fertilizers is highly effective for improving soil health and maize productivity.

For complete, location-specific recommendations based on local ecology and soil type, please read  the 2016 Ethiopian Calendar (E.C.) prepared maize Guideline on synthetic and organic fertilizer.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - The main fungal leaf diseases affecting maize in E
documents.text:
The main fungal leaf diseases affecting maize in Ethiopia are Gray Leaf Spot, Phaeosphaeria Leaf Spot, Turcicum Leaf Blight, and common Maize Leaf Rust. In Ethiopian the most of improved maize varieties are resistant to these diseases. The primary and most effective prevention method is planting improved varieties that are resistant to these diseases.
As there are currently no MLN-resistant varieties in Ethiopia, prevention through integrated management is critical:
Maintain strict field and environmental sanitation.
Use only certified, disease-free seed.
Control weeds in and around the field, as they can host the insect vectors (pests) that transmit the virus.
Enhance crop vigor by applying manure, compost, and fertilizer.
Practice crop rotation.
Since plants infected with MLN cannot be cured, immediately uproot and burn or deeply bury any symptomatic plants to stop the disease from spreading.
For Maize Head Smut, the key control strategy is to use chemically treated seed alongside other integrated disease management practices.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - Maize has little ability to compete with weeds dur
documents.text:
Maize has little ability to compete with weeds during its early growth stages. While manual weeding is ideal, an integrated approach is most effective.
First Weeding: Conduct when the crop is at the 3-leaf stage, approximately 18–25 days after sprouting.
Second Weeding: Perform 35–40 days after sprouting, ideally just before the second application of Urea fertilizer.
Third Weeding: If necessary, a third weeding can be done 50–55 days after sprouting.
Dry plowing during field preparation.
Sowing at the proper depth in straight rows.
Rotating maize with legume crops.
If herbicide use becomes necessary:
Pre-Emergence Herbicides:
Apply within three days after sowing while the soil is moist. Recommended products include Primagram Gold 660 SC, Surestart SE, or Integrity.
Example dosage for Primagram Gold 660 SC: Dilute 3 liters of herbicide in 200 liters of water per hectare.
Post-Emergence Herbicides:
For weeds that emerge with the crop, products such as 2,4-D 720 g/l A.E or Auxo EC 337 can be used.
Before using any herbicide, read the label instructions carefully and consult your local agricultural expert to ensure proper and safe application.
Integrated Weed Management is the most effective strategy combines good field preparation, using clean seed, timely manual weeding, and the judicious use of pre- and post-emergence herbicides.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - Recommendations for fertilizer use in maize cultiv
documents.text:
Recommendations for fertilizer use in maize cultivation depend on many local factors and vary from place to place. Farmers should consult their local Kebele or Woreda agricultural extension expert to determine the specific type and amount of fertilizer needed for their soil.
The following section offers general guidance on fertilizer timing to increase production.
Fertilizer Timing
In the early sprout stage, the nutrient demand of maize is low due to the plant's small size.
The critical period for nutrient availability begins within 20–25 days after sowing, when the number of kernel rows is being determined. Therefore:
First Application: It is preferable to apply the first dose of fertilizer (e.g., NPS and a portion of Urea) within this 20–25 day window.
Second Application: Apply the second dose (typically the remainder of the Urea) around 40–45 days after sowing.
Nutrient demand peaks around 50 days after sowing. A third application can be beneficial if applied between 50–60 days.
After approximately 60 days, the plant's demand for fertilizer begins to decline.
Key Principle: Ensuring the crop has no nutrient deficiency during this entire 20–60 day period is crucial for achieving high yields.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - The maize crop should be harvested when fully matu
documents.text:
The maize crop should be harvested when fully mature, indicated when a black layer forms at the point where the kernel attaches to the cob (visible when the kernel tip is scraped off). At this stage, grain moisture content typically reaches 13–18%.
Post-Harvest Process
Dehusking: Remove the husks from each cob.
Drying: Place the dehusked cobs in a drying area.
Shelling: Using a maize shelling machine is recommended for efficiency. Shell the kernels from the cob.
Final Drying: Continue drying the shelled kernels until the moisture content is reduced to the safe storage level of 13–14%.
Storage: Only then should the grain be placed in the granary for storage
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - Before threshing or shelling, the dehusked maize m
documents.text:
Before threshing or shelling, the dehusked maize must be properly dried. Spread the cobs on a clean, dry surface (such as a tarp) until the grain moisture content reaches 13–14%. A practical test is to bite a kernel; it should make a distinct "crunch" sound and feel hard.

To save time and labor and ensure grain quality, it is recommended to use an improved multi-purpose shelling machine. These can be pedal-operated, motor-driven, or another improved type, and can process a few cobs or many at a time. Farmers are advised to buy or rent such equipment for optimal results.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Maize Production - After maize has been shelled and dried to a 12-13%
documents.text:
After maize has been shelled and dried to a 12-13% moisture level, it must be stored in a secure granary or container. The storage must be airtight, moisture-proof, and free from holes to protect against rain, rodents, and other pests.

To maintain quality for food or market, store the grain in hermetic plastic bags or another type of storage that does not absorb moisture.

For long-term storage (more than one month), preventive pesticide treatment is recommended. Options include:

Treating the grain with the pesticide Actellic or Ethiolathion.

If pesticides are unavailable, an alternative method is to mix the maize with teff or finger millet.

If pests still appear despite these methods, the granary should be fumigated periodically using a pesticide confirmed as appropriate for this purpose.
documents.source: Maize Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Introduction
documents.text:
Crop Production Stage:
Key activities carried out to increase crop productivity
Content of the message to be transmitted to the farmer through the Interactive Voice Response (IVR) system
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Field Preparation
documents.text:
To get information about field preparation, press 1.
You must prepare your teff field to ensure the soil has sufficient fertility and moisture. Adequate soil moisture at the time of sowing is essential for increasing teff yields.
After harvest, plow the field in the autumn to a depth of 20 to 30 centimeters while the soil still retains its moisture.
If your teff field has a fine, loose tilth, plow it 2 to 3 times. If the soil is unplowed or virgin land, plow it 3 to 5 times thoroughly.
When using a tractor, plowing twice is typically sufficient. It is then advisable to follow this with a third, lighter pass using oxen to refine the seedbed.
To achieve optimal soil conditioning and pulverization:
Conduct the second plowing 3 to 4 weeks after the first.
Conduct the third plowing 3 weeks after the second.
Apply crop residue (as mulch or compost) to the field. This practice minimizes soil erosion and significantly enhances soil fertility, structure, and moisture retention capacity.For sowing teff, the soil should have a fine, crumbly tilth—similar to beso (roasted barley flour). It should hold together lightly when squeezed but break apart easily when disturbed.
In soils prone to crusting or surface hardening, applying light compaction after sowing improves teff germination, helps the soil retain moisture longer, and suppresses weeds by drying them out and limiting their spread.
To ensure effective integration of soil and fertilizer, follow this sequence after the final pre-sowing plowing:
Distribute the teff seeds evenly across the prepared surface.
Apply the recommended fertilizer.
Perform a final, light compaction to incorporate the seeds and fertilizer into the top layer of soil.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - On Teff Varieties
documents.text:
To get information about  on teff varieties, press 2.
Variety Selection Guidelines:
For areas with medium to long maturity periods, sow: Bishoftu, Eba, Bereket, Kule, Jitu, Jarso, Washerra, Aksumite, Biradama, Kuncho, Etsub, Kora, Dagim, or other teff varieties with similar characteristics.
For lowland, drought-prone, and early-maturing areas, use: Boset, Boni, Bora, Mena, Tsedey, Mechara, Gerado, or Simada.
For highland areas prone to waterlogging, use: Gimbichu and Dega Teff.
For irrigated cultivation, use the variety: Bekur.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Sowing Methods
documents.text:
To get information on seeding  rates, press 1.
The recommended seeding rate for improved teff varieties is 10 to 15 kilograms per hectare.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Sowing Methods
documents.text:
To get information on planting depth and method, press 2.
To facilitate row sowing of teff, use the furrowing ("dirdaro") method. When sowing in rows, maintain a spacing of 20 centimeters between rows.
Recommended Procedure for Ox-Plowed Land:
After final land preparation, create furrows.
Apply NPS fertilizer by dripping it along the bottom of the furrow.
On the return pass with the oxen, use a light implement to cover the fertilizer with a thin layer of soil.
In the same furrow (now with covered fertilizer), create a small, shallow seed furrow.
Sow the teff seeds in this shallow furrow using a row seeder (Meftihe) or a modified seeder (e.g., made from a "Highland" water bottle).
Do not cover the seeds after sowing. Subsequent rainfall will naturally wash soil from the sides of the furrow, covering the seed base and aiding seedling establishment.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Sowing Methods
documents.text:
To get information on transplanting seedlings, press 3.
Transplanting teff seedlings is recommended for fields prone to waterlogging and muddiness. This method increases productivity compared to direct seeding and provides significant protection against weed infestation.
Seedling Preparation:
Prepare seedlings by spreading and germinating 0.5 kg of teff seed on a nursery or prepared land. This amount is sufficient to produce seedlings for transplanting across one hectare.
Transplanting Timing:
For highland (dega) areas: Transplant seedlings to the main field 25–28 days after sowing in the nursery.
For lowland (kola) areas: Transplant seedlings to the main field 18–20 days after nursery sowing, or when seedlings have developed 3–4 true leaves.
Transplanting Method:
Maintain a spacing of 20 cm between rows and 10 cm between plants within the row.
Plant three seedlings per hole.
Work backward across the field to avoid compacting the freshly transplanted area.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Sowing Methods
documents.text:
To get information on moisture retention, press 4.
Moisture retention refers to the soil's ability to absorb and hold water, making it available to plants over time. Effective moisture management is critical for teff, especially in semi-arid regions or during erratic rainfall.
1. Key Practices to Improve Moisture Retention:
Organic Matter Application:Incorporate well-decomposed compost, manure, or crop residues (mulch) into the soil.
Conservation Tillage: Minimize unnecessary plowing. Consider zero-till or minimum-till practices where appropriate.
Post-Planting Light Compaction: On loose, sandy, or crust-prone soils, apply light compaction (e.g., with a roller) after sowing seeds and fertilizer.
Use of Mulches: After sowing, apply a thin layer of organic mulch (e.g., straw) if available.
Contour Plowing and Bunding: On sloped land, always plow and form planting rows along the contour (horizontal lines following the slope).
Timely Weeding:Control weeds early and consistently.
2. Soil-Specific Advice:
Sandy Soils: Have low water-holding capacity. Priority: Add large quantities of organic matter and use mulches.
Clay Soils: Hold more water but have slow infiltration. Priority: Add organic matter to improve structure and prevent cracking.
Loamy Soils: Ideal. Maintain their structure with regular organic inputs and avoid compaction.
3. Critical Timing for Moisture:
Germination & Seedling Stage: The top 3-5 cm of soil must remain consistently moist. This is the most critical period for irrigation or reliant on rainfall.
Tillering & Stem Elongation: Adequate soil moisture is essential for strong root and shoot development.
Heading & Grain Filling: Moisture stress during this stage severely reduces yield. Ensure deep soil moisture is available.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Sowing Methods
documents.text:
To get information on  fertilizer application, press 5.
Teff Fertilizer Application Guidelines
A. Synthetic Fertilizer Recommendations
1. Basal Application (NPS or NPSB):
Soil Type: Black soils (Vertisols).
Rate: 100–150 kg (2–3 bags of 50 kg) per hectare.
Timing & Method: Apply on the day of sowing. Place the fertilizer at a depth of 3–5 cm within the prepared sowing row or furrow and cover with soil before seeding.
2. Top-Dressing (Urea - 46-0-0):
For Heavy ("Merere") Soils: Apply 100–150 kg (2–3 bags) per hectare.
For Light Soils (Red/Brown): Apply 50–100 kg (1–2 bags) per hectare.
Application Method for Urea (Split Application):
First Application: Apply half of the recommended urea rate at the base of the plants 15–18 days after sowing (DAS). Incorporate lightly into the soil and cover.
Second Application: Apply the remaining half 35–40 DAS. Incorporate lightly and cover.
3. Special Cases:
Acidic Soils: Consult local agricultural experts for specific fertilizer types (e.g., lime, modified NPS) and precise application timing.
Following a Pulse Crop: If the previous crop was a legume (pulse) that was properly cultivated, mowed (not uprooted) at harvest, and residues were left in the field, you can reduce the urea requirement by 50%. Example Rate: 100 kg/ha NPS + 50 kg/ha Urea.
Following Niger Seed (Nug): Reduce the nitrogen (Urea) rate by one-third (use only two-thirds of the standard recommendation) if Niger seed was cultivated in the field the previous year.
B. Organic Fertilizer Recommendations
Timing: Apply well-decomposed manure or compost 3–4 weeks before sowing to allow for proper integration.
Rates:
Sole Application (Organic only): 20–30 metric quintals (2,000–3,000 kg) per hectare.
Integrated Application (Organic + Synthetic): Apply 15–20 quintals (1,500–2,000 kg) of organic fertilizer per hectare combined with 50–75 kg per hectare of synthetic NPS fertilizer.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Fertilizer Preparation
documents.text:
To get information about fertilizer preparation, press 4.
Information on fertilizer application / Fertilizer addition method.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Pest Control
documents.text:
To get information about pest control, press 1.
Teff Pest Management Guidelines
Major Insect Pests:
The primary insect pests of teff are the Teff Shoot Fly, Red Teff Worm (Armyworm), Grasshoppers, and related species.
1. Cultural and Preventive Control (Recommended First Steps):Implement these practices to reduce pest populations and damage:
Early and Deep Plowing: Plow fields early in the season and to a sufficient depth to expose pest larvae and pupae to sunlight and predators.
Post-Harvest Plowing: Plow immediately after harvest to disrupt the life cycle of remaining pests.
Crop Rotation: Rotate teff with non-host crops (e.g., pulses) to break pest cycles.
Sanitation: Remove alternative host plants and weeds from field margins and surrounding areas.
Use Certified Chemicals: If chemical control becomes necessary, always select registered pesticides that are within their expiration date.
2. Curative Chemical Control (Use as a Last Resort):
Apply pesticides only when pest scouting indicates that infestation levels exceed economic thresholds. Always follow label instructions precisely.
For Teff Shoot Fly:
Furadan 5G (Carbofuran): Use as a seed treatment at 0.9 kg active ingredient per hectare (Note: Availability and registration of this product should be verified with local authorities).
Alternative: Apply a registered foliar insecticide at the first sign of damage.
For Red Teff Worm (Armyworm):
Carbaryl 85% WP: Apply at 1.5 kg per hectare.
Method: Dilute in water according to the specified ratio on the product label and spray thoroughly. 3.
3. Critical Advisory:
Safety First: Wear proper protective equipment (PPE) when handling and applying pesticides.
Seek Expert Advice: Always consult your local Kebele agricultural expert for the most current, registered product recommendations, precise application rates, and safe handling procedures before using any pesticide.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Disease Control
documents.text:
To get information about disease control, press 2.
To protect teff from disease, use resistant varieties, practice crop rotation, and apply fungicides only after consulting agricultural experts.
Main Teff Diseases:
The primary diseases affecting teff are:
Leaf Rust ("wag")
Head Smut ("eras-akoshish" / "atkur")
Damping-off
Preventive Measures:
For Head Smut and damping-off, the most effective prevention is to use certified, disease-resistant varieties and clean, treated seed.
Leaf Rust and Head Smut can spread rapidly and cause significant crop loss. Regularly scout your field to detect early signs of infection.
Curative Control (Fungicide Application):
If scouting confirms the presence of Leaf Rust or Head Smut at an alarming level, take immediate action.
Consult your local agricultural expert to select an appropriate fungicide. Registered options may include Nativo 300 SC, Rex Duo, Fusaro 400 SC, Natura 250 EW, Tilt 250 EC, or equivalents.
Apply the fungicide according to the recommended dosage and safety instructions on the product label.
Timing and Repeat Application: Considering the crop's growth stage, apply the first spray as soon as the disease is confirmed. Repeat the application 1–2 more times at 14- to 21-day intervals, especially as the teff approaches and reaches the flowering stage, for effective control.
Safety and Compliance:
Always wear proper chemical protective gear (gloves, mask, goggles, etc.) when mixing and spraying fungicides.
Strictly follow the dosage, dilution ratio, and safety instructions on the product container.
For specific product recommendations and application guidance, always consult the agricultural experts in your area.
To get information about weeds management, press 3.Effective weed control in teff integrates cultural, mechanical, and chemical methods..
1. Cultural & Preventive Measures:
Crop Rotation: Rotate teff with non-cereal crops (e.g., pulses) to disrupt weed life cycles.
Use Clean Inputs: Sow certified teff seed that is free from weed seeds.
Sanitize Equipment: Clean farming tools and machinery before moving between fields to prevent weed seed spread.
Clean Harvest: At harvest, mow weeds and the teff crop separately. Thresh in a designated, clean area to avoid contaminating grain with weed seeds.
2. Mechanical (Hand) Weeding:
First Weeding: Perform hand weeding 15–18 days after crop emergence.
Second Weeding: Perform a second weeding 25–30 days after emergence. This should be completed before applying the top-dressing Urea fertilizer.
3. Chemical Control (Herbicide Use ):
This is the last option and herbicides should only be considered when cultural and mechanical methods are insufficient.
Product: Pallas OD 45 (Pyroxsulam) is a selective, post-emergence herbicide.
Application Window: Spray 25–30 days after sowing, when both the teff crop and weeds are at the recommended growth stage.
Rate: Dilute 0.4 liters (400 ml) of the herbicide in clean water per hectare, as per label instructions.
Spectrum: Controls a broad spectrum of both broadleaf and grass weeds.
Critical Advisory: Always consult your local agricultural expert before use. They can confirm the herbicide’s suitability for your field, provide precise mixing and application instructions, and advise on safety procedures.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Harvesting
documents.text:
To get information about harvesting, press 1.
Harvest teff when the color of the grain panicle and stalk turns yellowish or pale—before it becomes overly dry. A good indicator is that the teff grain produces a distinct "crunch" sound when bitten with the teeth.
After cutting, spread the harvested crop on a dry field for approximately three days, or as local conditions dictate, to allow the straw to dry thoroughly.
If the teff was established by transplanting seedlings, use a motorized mower for harvesting. This method helps minimize grain loss from shattering.
Once the crop is harvested and dried, proper storage is essential before threshing. Collect and heap the teff on a raised platform (bed) constructed above the ground. This practice protects the harvest from damage caused by rain and surface floodwater.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Threshing And Cleaning
documents.text:
To get information about threshing and cleaning, press 2.
The preferred method for threshing teff is to use a mechanical threshing machine.
If a machine is not available, you may thresh using oxen on a properly prepared threshing floor. The floor must be cleanly prepared and smeared (e.g., with dung or mud) to create a smooth, hard surface.
Using these methods protects your teff from unnecessary waste and preserves grain quality.
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture

documents.name: Teff - Post-Harvest Handling
documents.text:
To get information about post-harvest handling, press 3.
Use a clean, airtight storage container or granary that is free from holes, cracks, and pests. The storage area must be dry and protected from moisture, humidity, and rain.
The moisture content of the teff grain must not exceed 13% before storage.
Remove all dirt, chaff, weed seeds, and other foreign materials from the grain.
Sort and store the teff separately by grade based on quality, color, time of production, and other relevant characteristics.
I  pre-planting to pre-sowing
The content part is empty…..both in the original and translated documents…?
The content part is empty…..both in the original and translated documents…?
Revisit the Amharic version. The English version is correct. The Amharic should have been ‘ተክል"
documents.source: Teff Production Guide - Ministry of Agriculture
